content.text: What Is an Azure Region Pair?

Every Azure region is paired with exactly one other region within the same geography. The pair is Microsoft's built-in cross-region resilience mechanism: if one region in a pair suffers a catastrophic failure — a natural disaster, wide-area power outage, or multi-zone datacenter event — the paired region becomes the recovery target for services that have been configured for geo-replication.

Region pairs are not chosen arbitrarily. Microsoft selects pairs based on physical distance (typically 300+ miles, large enough to survive regional natural disasters), shared geography (so data residency requirements are not violated during failover), and network topology (the pair is connected by a high-bandwidth private fibre link that supports low-latency asynchronous replication).

Region pairs are a platform-level resilience commitment from Microsoft, not merely a customer configuration option. They determine how Microsoft coordinates planned maintenance, where geo-redundant storage replicates data, and which region Azure Site Recovery uses as the default DR target.

attrs.alt: Azure Region Pair Architecture: primary region with three Availability Zones connected via GRS async replication to a paired region 300+ miles away, with automatic protections at the bottom

content.text: The architecture above illustrates the relationship between Availability Zones (within-region resilience) and region pairs (cross-region resilience). Zone-redundant deployments survive single-datacentre failures within the primary region. Region pairs handle the case where the entire primary region is unavailable — an event rare enough that Microsoft's SLA does not guarantee zero-downtime across region failures, but common enough in large-scale infrastructure that it must be planned for.
What Azure Guarantees for Region Pairs

Azure continuously deploys platform updates — OS patches, hypervisor upgrades, fabric controller changes — to its infrastructure. For unpaired regions, Microsoft could theoretically update both regions simultaneously, risking a regression that disables both simultaneously. For paired regions, Microsoft guarantees that both regions in a pair are never in a planned maintenance window at the same time. If East US enters a maintenance window, West US is excluded from maintenance during that window.
Priority Recovery in a Declared Disaster

If a large-scale disaster affects multiple Azure regions simultaneously, Microsoft triages recovery efforts. For each region pair, Microsoft guarantees that at least one of the two regions will be prioritised for recovery. This means that in the worst-case scenario — total loss of a region — customers who have deployed geo-redundant services to the paired region have a higher probability of service restoration within the declared disaster period.

When you create an Azure Storage account with Geo-Redundant Storage (GRS) or Geo-Zone-Redundant Storage (GZRS), data is asynchronously replicated to the paired region. The target region is always the region pair — you cannot choose an arbitrary secondary region for GRS. This means that if East US is your primary, West US is your GRS secondary regardless of your preference.

Azure Site Recovery (ASR) — the platform's disaster recovery orchestration service — uses the region pair as its default replication target when you configure VM replication. You can override this to a non-paired region if your requirements demand it, but the default assumes the pairing logic.

Some regions have asymmetric GRS: Brazil South's paired region (Brazil Southeast) is also paired with Brazil South, but in a region pair list Brazil South may also list South Central US as an older pairing in some documentation. Always check the current Azure documentation for the definitive pairing for your target region, as pairings are occasionally updated as new regions come online.
GRS vs GZRS: Choosing Your Storage Redundancy Model
The two geo-redundant storage SKUs combine region pair replication with different within-region redundancy:

# Create a GZRS storage account — zone-redundant primary + geo-redundant secondary
az storage account create \
  --name prodstggzrs \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Standard_GZRS \          # Geo-Zone-Redundant Storage
  --kind StorageV2 \
  --access-tier Hot

# Initiate a read-access GRS account (RA-GZRS) — secondary region is readable
# Note: RA-GZRS means data in West US can be read even before failover
az storage account create \
  --name prodstgragzrs \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Standard_RAGZRS \         # Read-Access Geo-Zone-Redundant
  --kind StorageV2

# Check replication status of an account
az storage account show \
  --name prodstggzrs \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --query "{SKU:sku.name, GeoStatus:secondaryLocation, LastSync:geoReplicationStats.lastSyncTime}" \
  --output json

RA-GRS and RA-GZRS are the read-access variants — the secondary region's data is readable at all times via a secondary endpoint (accountname-secondary.blob.core.windows.net). This allows read traffic to be offloaded to the paired region, and also means application code can be tested against the secondary before a real failover occurs.
Azure SQL Geo-Replication and Failover Groups

Azure SQL Database and SQL Managed Instance implement region pair resilience through active geo-replication and failover groups. Failover groups provide an automatic, readable secondary in the paired region with DNS-based failover so the application connection string does not change.

# Create a SQL Database failover group between East US (primary) and West US (secondary)

# Step 1: Create the primary server and database
az sql server create \
  --name prod-sql-primary \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --location eastus \
  --admin-user sqladmin \
  --admin-password "Str0ngPas$word!"

az sql db create \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --server prod-sql-primary \
  --name appdb \
  --tier GeneralPurpose \
  --family Gen5 \
  --capacity 4 \
  --zone-redundant true          # Zone-redundant within East US

# Step 2: Create the secondary server in the paired region (West US)
az sql server create \
  --name prod-sql-secondary \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --location westus \
  --admin-user sqladmin \
  --admin-password "Str0ngPas$word!"

# Step 3: Create a failover group (creates the geo-replica automatically)
az sql failover-group create \
  --name prod-fog \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --server prod-sql-primary \
  --partner-server prod-sql-secondary \
  --failover-policy Automatic \         # Auto-failover on RPO breach
  --grace-period 1 \                    # 1-hour grace before auto-failover
  --add-db appdb

# Application connection strings (don't change on failover):
#   Read-write: prod-fog.database.windows.net        → always points to primary
#   Read-only:  prod-fog.secondary.database.windows.net → always points to secondary
Configuring Azure Site Recovery for VM Failover

Azure Site Recovery (ASR) replicates entire VMs (including OS disks, data disks, and network configuration) to the paired region. On failover, ASR can boot the replicated VM in the secondary region within minutes, using the latest replicated checkpoint.

# Enable ASR replication for a VM (East US → West US)

# Step 1: Create a Recovery Services vault in the paired region
az backup vault create \
  --resource-group dr-rg \
  --name prod-rsv \
  --location westus \
  --redundancy GeoRedundant

# Step 2: Enable replication for a specific VM
# (ASR replication is managed through ARM templates or the Portal for full config)
# This shows the CLI approach for the basic settings:
az site-recovery replicated-item create \
  --resource-group dr-rg \
  --vault-name prod-rsv \
  --name "rep-app-vm-01" \
  --provider-specific-details '{
    "instanceType": "InMageAzureV2",
    "targetResourceGroupId": "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/dr-rg",
    "targetNetworkId": "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/dr-rg/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/dr-vnet",
    "targetSubnetName": "default",
    "targetVmSize": "Standard_D4s_v5"
  }'

# Step 3: Run a test failover (non-destructive — spins up the VM in an isolated network)
az site-recovery replicated-item planned-failover \
  --resource-group dr-rg \
  --vault-name prod-rsv \
  --replicated-item-name "rep-app-vm-01" \
  --failover-direction PrimaryToRecovery \
  --provider-specific-details '{"instanceType":"InMageAzureV2"}'

A test failover is the only way to verify that ASR replication is working correctly and that the replicated VM boots and responds to health checks. Run a full DR drill — including DNS cutover and application smoke tests against the secondary region — at least once per year.
Cosmos DB Multi-Region Writes Using the Region Pair

Cosmos DB supports active-active multi-region writes where both the primary and paired secondary accept write traffic simultaneously. This eliminates write latency for users in both regions and provides continuous availability even if one region fails — no failover step is required.

# Create a Cosmos DB account with multi-region writes in East US + West US pair
az cosmosdb create \
  --name prod-cosmos \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --locations regionName=eastus failoverPriority=0 isZoneRedundant=true \
  --locations regionName=westus failoverPriority=1 isZoneRedundant=true \
  --default-consistency-level Session \
  --enable-multiple-write-locations true \    # ACTIVE-ACTIVE: both regions accept writes
  --enable-automatic-failover false            # Not needed with multi-write — already active-active

# Verify the account replication configuration
az cosmosdb show \
  --name prod-cosmos \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --query "{Locations:locations[].{Region:locationName,Priority:failoverPriority,ZR:isZoneRedundant}, MultiWrite:enableMultipleWriteLocations}" \
  --output json

Multi-region writes use the Conflict Resolution Policy to handle concurrent writes to the same document from both regions. The default is Last-Write-Wins based on server timestamp (_ts). For financial or inventory workloads where conflict resolution matters, custom merge procedures can be registered.
Region Pairs and Compliance: Data Residency During Failover

When an application fails over from its primary to the paired region, all data and processing moves to that paired region. For workloads subject to data residency requirements (GDPR, UK GDPR, sovereign cloud mandates), this means the paired region must also satisfy the same regulatory constraints.
Both regions in EU pairs (North Europe / West Europe; UK South / UK West; Germany WC / Germany North) are in the same regulatory jurisdiction. GDPR compliance is maintained through failover.
Azure Government region pairs (USGov Virginia / USGov Texas; USGov Arizona / USDoD Central) are all within US federal cloud boundary. FedRAMP compliance is maintained through failover.

Brazil South pairs with Brazil Southeast — both in Brazil. However, for older documentation you may see Brazil South → South Central US listed. If deployed to this older pairing, GRS failover would move data to the US, violating Brazilian data residency law. Verify the current pairing for every compliance-sensitive deployment.
Australia East / Australia Southeast are both in Australia and satisfy Australian Privacy Act requirements through failover.

GRS is asynchronous: data is replicated to the paired region asynchronously, meaning there is a lag (Recovery Point Objective). For Azure Storage, the typical RPO is less than 15 minutes, but during network saturation it can be higher. For RPO-sensitive workloads, use Cosmos DB multi-region writes (RPO = 0) or SQL Database active geo-replication with synchronous commit.

Failover requires customer action for most services: GRS storage does not auto-failover — a customer-initiated failover is required via Portal, CLI, or ARM API. Only Cosmos DB (with automatic failover enabled) and SQL Database failover groups (with Automatic policy) can fail over without human action.

Region pairs are not always symmetric in service availability: UK West (Wales) — the pair for UK South — does not offer all services available in UK South. If your architecture uses a service only available in the primary region, the failover plan must account for this gap.

Egress charges during replication: data replicated across regions via GRS or SQL geo-replication incurs outbound bandwidth charges from the primary region. At 1 TB/month, East US to West US egress costs approximately $50/month.

Testing failover modifies primary state: for storage account failover (not test failover), the secondary becomes the new primary and the old primary is degraded to secondary with LRS. After failover, you must re-enable GRS to restore cross-region redundancy.
Initiating a Storage Account Failover to the Paired Region

When your primary storage region is unavailable and you need to promote the secondary to primary, Azure provides a customer-initiated failover mechanism. After failover, the secondary becomes the new primary (LRS in the former secondary region) and the original primary — once recovered — becomes the secondary. You must re-enable geo-redundancy after failover to restore the full GRS/GZRS protection.

# Check replication lag before deciding to fail over
az storage account show \
  --name prodstggzrs \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --query "{LastSync:geoReplicationStats.lastSyncTime, Status:geoReplicationStats.status, SecondaryLocation:secondaryLocation}" \
  --output json

# Initiate account failover (irreversible until you re-enable GRS)
# This promotes West US secondary to primary; East US becomes LRS secondary after recovery
az storage account failover \
  --name prodstggzrs \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --no-wait false     # Wait for failover to complete

# After primary region recovers, re-enable geo-replication to restore GZRS
# The recovered East US region will now become the secondary
az storage account update \
  --name prodstggzrs \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --sku Standard_GZRS

# Monitor failover completion
az storage account show \
  --name prodstggzrs \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --query "{Location:primaryLocation, SKU:sku.name, GeoStatus:secondaryLocation}" \
  --output json

The geo-replication status field before failover reads 'Live' (healthy) or 'Unavailable' (primary unreachable). The lastSyncTime field shows the most recent successful replication timestamp — data written after this time may not be in the secondary. This represents the actual RPO for the failover decision.

Geo-replication lag is the time gap between a write landing in the primary and being confirmed durable in the secondary. Azure Storage publishes the last successful sync timestamp; SQL Database and Cosmos DB expose replication lag as a metric in Azure Monitor.

# Storage: check last sync time across multiple accounts in a subscription
az storage account list \
  --query "[?sku.name=='Standard_GZRS' || sku.name=='Standard_GRS'].{Name:name, LastSync:geoReplicationStats.lastSyncTime, Status:geoReplicationStats.status}" \
  --output table

# Azure SQL: monitor geo-replication lag via sys.dm_geo_replication_link_status
# (run from the primary SQL server)
# SELECT * FROM sys.dm_geo_replication_link_status;
# Key columns:
#   redo_queue_size        = outstanding replication lag in KB
#   replication_lag_sec    = seconds behind primary
#   link_state             = 'CATCH_UP' | 'SEEDING' | 'SUSPENDED'

# Azure Monitor: set an alert when SQL geo-replication lag exceeds 60 seconds
az monitor metrics alert create \
  --name "sql-geo-lag-alert" \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --scopes /subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/prod-rg/providers/Microsoft.Sql/servers/prod-sql-primary \
  --condition "avg replication_lag_sec > 60" \
  --evaluation-frequency 5m \
  --window-size 15m \
  --severity 2 \
  --action-groups /subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/prod-rg/providers/Microsoft.Insights/actionGroups/ops-ag
Cross-Region Networking: Connecting Primary and Paired Region

Applications that span the primary and paired region — active-passive setups, read-replica patterns, cross-region backup verification — need secure connectivity between the two regions. Azure provides three options:
Most workloads; uses private Microsoft network
ExpressRoute circuit cost + Global Reach fee

# Connect East US and West US VNets via Global VNet Peering (uses Microsoft backbone)
# This enables the paired region app tier to communicate with the primary DB without the internet

# Peer East US VNet → West US VNet
az network vnet peering create \
  --name eastus-to-westus \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --vnet-name prod-vnet-eastus \
  --remote-vnet /subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/dr-rg/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/dr-vnet-westus \
  --allow-vnet-access \
  --allow-forwarded-traffic

# Peer West US VNet → East US VNet (peering is not symmetric — must create both directions)
az network vnet peering create \
  --name westus-to-eastus \
  --resource-group dr-rg \
  --vnet-name dr-vnet-westus \
  --remote-vnet /subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/prod-rg/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/prod-vnet-eastus \
  --allow-vnet-access \
  --allow-forwarded-traffic

# Check peering state (must be 'Connected' on both sides)
az network vnet peering list \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --vnet-name prod-vnet-eastus \
  --query "[].{Name:name, State:peeringState, RemoteVnet:remoteVirtualNetwork.id}" \
  --output table

VNet peering across region pairs uses Microsoft's private global backbone — traffic never traverses the public internet. This provides security equivalent to a private link and latency far lower than routing through an internet gateway.
DR Runbook Template: Region Pair Failover
A well-designed DR runbook removes ambiguity during an incident. The following template covers the decision points and commands for a failover from East US to West US (the region pair):

Confirm the outage: verify via the Azure Service Health dashboard (portal.azure.com/#blade/Microsoft_Azure_Health) that East US is experiencing a service disruption, not just a monitoring artifact. Check the Azure Status page at status.azure.com.

Assess data lag: run 'az storage account show --query geoReplicationStats.lastSyncTime' for all storage accounts and check SQL Database replication_lag_sec. Document the RPO at decision time — data written after lastSyncTime may be lost.
Notify stakeholders: alert the incident management team, business owners, and engineering leads with the assessed RPO. Get explicit authorisation to proceed with failover.
Fail over storage accounts: run 'az storage account failover' for each production GRS/GZRS account. Wait for completion before proceeding.

Fail over SQL failover groups: for each SQL failover group, initiate failover — 'az sql failover-group set-primary --name <fog-name> --server prod-sql-secondary'. The secondary becomes primary; the DNS listener switches automatically.
Start standby VMs in West US: scale up the warm standby VM instances. If using ASR, initiate the failover runbook: 'az site-recovery replicated-item failover'.
Update DNS / Traffic Manager: if using Traffic Manager with manual failover, disable the East US endpoint to force traffic to West US endpoint.
Run smoke tests: validate application functionality in West US. Check health endpoints, database connectivity, and key user journeys.
Monitor and communicate: post status updates every 15 minutes. When stable, send the all-clear and begin planning recovery back to East US when the primary region recovers.

A DR runbook is only useful if it has been tested. Run a full drill — including actual failover of non-critical environments — at least twice per year. A drill that has never fired a real 'az storage account failover' has not been tested.
When Not to Use the Default Region Pair
The default region pair is the right choice for most workloads, but certain scenarios justify using a non-paired secondary:

If your primary is in East US and most of your users are in Europe, you might prefer West Europe as a secondary (closer to users) rather than West US. Cosmos DB multi-region and SQL Database active geo-replication support non-paired regions.
Capacity guarantee in both regions simultaneously:

The region pair guarantee that 'at least one is prioritised in disaster' could mean the paired region (not yours) gets prioritised. For mission-critical workloads, some organisations deploy to three regions (primary + two secondaries) to ensure at least one secondary always has capacity.

Cosmos DB supports unlimited region replicas. An e-commerce platform might deploy to East US, West Europe, and Southeast Asia — none of which are traditional region pairs — to minimise global latency and maximise resilience.
A workload where some data must remain in UK and other data is global might use UK South as primary, UK West as the UK-compliant secondary, and North Europe as a non-sensitive global replica.

Azure region pairs are the foundational cross-region resilience mechanism in the Azure platform. Each region is paired with one geographically separated partner within the same compliance boundary. Microsoft coordinates planned maintenance to avoid simultaneous downtime, and in a declared disaster prioritises recovery of at least one region in each pair. Geo-Redundant Storage (GRS/GZRS) replicates data to the paired region asynchronously; SQL Database failover groups and Cosmos DB multi-region writes extend this pattern to relational and NoSQL data. Designing for region pairs — by choosing GZRS storage, SQL failover groups, ASR for VM replication, and Cosmos DB multi-write — ensures that an entire regional failure triggers a controlled, well-understood failover rather than an emergency recovery scramble.
